items.q: What are conventions of the Gothic genre?
items.correct: Settings of ruin and decay, supernatural or psychological terror, transgression of boundaries, the sublime, doubled or haunted protagonists, and atmospheric excess.
items.misconception: Gothic just means scary stories with vampires and ghosts.
items.random_wrong: Gothic refers to medieval architecture, not literature.
items.why: Students reduce Gothic to monsters. The genre is defined by its concerns — boundaries, the past intruding on the present, the limits of reason — and only sometimes uses supernatural creatures.

items.correct: Subtle hints or clues earlier in a text that anticipate later events, making the resolution feel both surprising and inevitable.
items.misconception: Foreshadowing is when a character predicts the future.
items.random_wrong: Foreshadowing is the shadow a character casts in a scene.
items.why: Foreshadowing works on the reader, not within the story world. A character's prediction is a plot event — foreshadowing is an authorial technique embedded in detail and tone.

items.q: Why are genre conventions important when interpreting a text?
items.correct: They set reader expectations the text can fulfil or subvert; analysing how a text uses or breaks conventions reveals its meaning and significance.
items.misconception: Genre is just a label to help bookshops categorise novels.
items.random_wrong: Genre means whether a book is fiction or non-fiction.
items.why: Genre is interpretively active. A horror novel that subverts horror conventions does so meaningfully — but only if you know the conventions. Students treat genre as labelling, not as a reading lens.

items.q: Who coined hegemony in its Marxist sense?
items.why: Correct answer: Gramsci. “Adorno” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Aura is associated with which thinker?
items.why: Correct answer: Walter Benjamin. “Theodor Adorno” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate decade the Frankfurt School was founded?
items.why: The real figure is 1920. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
